The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 discusses the literature review. Section 3 gives an overview of the study area and details the data used. Sections 4 and 5 outline the research methods and present the results, respectively. Finally, Section 6 provides the discussion and conclusion of this study. 

2. Literature review 

2.1. Healthcare accessibility studies for the older population 

The fundamental concept of spatial accessibility is how easily people can reach their destination (Geurs and van Wee, 2004; Neutens et al., 2010; Miller, 2018; Levinson and Wu, 2020; Siddiq and D. Taylor, 2021). Similarly, healthcare accessibility refers to the ease of reaching healthcare services (Polo et al., 2015). Although accessibility to healthcare can be evaluated through both spatial and non-spatial approaches, spatial accessibility has garnered significant research interest due to its influence on the utilization of services and the development of public health intervention strategies (Hawthorne and Kwan, 2013; Kim et al., 2018; Wang, 2012; Guagliardo, 2004). As a result, several techniques and tools have been introduced to understand spatial accessibility to healthcare services, including travel distance or time (Kelly et al., 2016), population ratios (Guagliardo, 2004), and gravity-based approaches, including a family of two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) methods (Wang, 2012). 

Ensuring adequate access to healthcare services is critical for aging populations, who often require frequent medical attention due to declining physical health associated with aging (Horton and Johnson, 2010; Dey et al., 2012). Recognizing this importance, researchers and practitioners have devoted substantial efforts to evaluating the spatial accessibility of healthcare services, particularly for older adults (Guida et al., 2022; Carpentieri et al., 2020; Jo et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2020; Tao and Cheng, 2019; Kang et al., 2023). These evaluations address various aspects, including accessibility to multi-tier care considering different transportation modes (Tao and Cheng, 2019; Carpentieri et al., 2020), supply-demand dynamics (Cheng et al., 2020), and identifying barriers that hinder access to healthcare services (Jo et al., 2021). However, a significant body of this research has focused on developed countries or countries with better healthcare infrastructure, such as China. 

In contrast, studies focusing on healthcare accessibility for the older population in developing countries or LMICs in South Asia have received relatively less attention. Most existing studies in these contexts either evaluate healthcare access for all age groups (Kim et al., 2023), do not comprehensively study access to multi-tier healthcare services (Tanser et al., 2006; Macharia et al., 2017), or focus on relatively small-scale study areas (Verma and Dash, 2020). In Bangladesh, several studies have examined the travel behaviour (Chudyk et al., 2015) and barriers faced by older individuals when accessing healthcare services (Hossen et al., 2023), as well as evaluated accessibility using both spatial (Abdullah et al., 2018) and non-spatial approaches (Sarker et al., 2023). Previous studies related to healthcare accessibility conducted in Bangladesh observed that primary care is mostly accessible to older citizens in rural and semi-urban areas. However, these studies highlight a lack of specialized medical staff and services necessary for treating chronic diseases (Abdullah et al., 2018), and high costs deter the older population from seeking specialized care (Biswas et al., 2006). Specifically, high out-of-pocket payments, a lack of caregivers, and time distance are primary barriers to healthcare accessibility in rural areas (Sarker et al., 2023), where cultural factors significantly affect older women’s access to healthcare (Hamiduzzaman et al., 2018). Therefore, an analysis of multi-tier healthcare services at a national scale, employing spatial accessibility methods and considering supply-demand dynamics (i.e., competition effect), could provide a more comprehensive understanding of areas with limited healthcare accessibility for the older population. 

2.2. Inequalities in healthcare accessibility for the older population 

The evaluation of inequality in healthcare accessibility has gained significant research attention in recent years (Lee and Kim, 2023; Javanmard et al., 2024; Sabokbar et al., 2021; Mayaud et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2022; Sharma and Patil, 2021). However, studies specifically examining healthcare accessibility disparities among older adults remain relatively limited, despite their distinct mobility constraints, higher medical needs, and greater dependence on healthcare services (Tao and Cheng, 2019; Cheng et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2023; Wu and Tseng, 2018). 

Tao and Cheng (2019) found significant disparities in healthcare accessibility for older adults in China, where access declines progressively from central urban areas to peripheral regions. Their study compared older and non-older adults, revealing that older adults face greater challenges in reaching healthcare services due to mobility limitations and lower proximity to healthcare facilities. Similarly, Cheng et al. (2020) examined the geographic accessibility of three-tier healthcare systems for older adults and found that accessibility decreases significantly for higher-tier services, exposing a clear equity gap. Expanding on this, Jin et al. (2023) investigated equity in elderly care institutions (ECIs) using the Gaussian two-step floating catchment area (Ga2SFCA) model and identified a mismatch between supply and demand, with existing facilities failing to meet the needs of aging populations. 

These studies collectively highlight that older adults face heightened inequalities in healthcare accessibility, shaped by transportation limitations, socioeconomic disparities, uneven facility distribution, and geographic barriers. The intersection of mobility constraints, financial limitations, and healthcare infrastructure gaps further exacerbates accessibility challenges, disproportionately affecting older adults with lower income levels and fewer transportation options. Addressing these disparities is crucial for developing age-friendly healthcare policies that ensure equitable access across different population groups. 

Most existing studies have focused on disparities in healthcare access within regions with well-developed healthcare in— frastructures. In contrast, lower-middle-income countries (LMICs) in regions such as South Asia and Africa including Bangladesh—have received limited scholarly attention, despite their rapidly aging populations and underdeveloped healthcare systems. As

LMICs undergo demographic shifts, understanding inequalities in healthcare access for older adults becomes increasingly critical. A deeper understanding of these disparities would enable policymakers in under-researched LMICs to develop targeted interventions and ensure adequate and equitable healthcare access for their aging populations. 

2.3. Spatial accessibility studies in Bangladesh 

Research on accessibility to facilities and resources in Bangladesh is limited, primarily due to the unavailability of requisite datasets. Existing studies in Bangladesh that evaluate accessibility often focus on specific services such as green spaces (Khan et al., 2023), cyclone shelters (Islam et al., 2023; Ahmed et al., 2025), and the housing market (Mitra and Saphores, 2016), as well as services like parks, schools, and healthcare (Ashik et al., 2020; Rahman et al., 2022). Notably, Ashik et al. (2020) measured accessibility to urban amenities from both spatial and aspatial perspectives, while Rahman et al. (2022) observed a correlation between spatial accessibility to urban services and transit-oriented development. To understand healthcare accessibility in the coastal region of Bangladesh, Zafri et al. (2021) investigated access to three levels of healthcare services—Community Clinics (CC), Upazila Health — Complexes (UHC), and District Hospitals (DH) using straightforward distance measures. They identified disparities among the different levels of healthcare. However, the variation in accessibility among primary, secondary, and tertiary services remains unexplored. Similarly, Ahmed et al. (2019) generated time-cost-based service areas to assess emergency health service accessibility for urban slum dwellers. 

Also, the existing research on spatial accessibility in Bangladesh predominantly focuses on Dhaka, the capital city, while other regions, including rural areas, have received minimal attention and remain widely understudied. Specifically, there has been limited focus on understanding inequalities in the spatial accessibility to healthcare services for older adults. Addressing this research gap, this study contributes to the growing body of knowledge on healthcare accessibility by assessing spatial accessibility to multi-tier healthcare services and examining inequalities for older individuals across Bangladesh. 

3. Study area and data 

3.1. Study area 

The study area for this research is Bangladesh, a rapidly aging society in the Global South (Fig. 1). Located in South Asia, Bangladesh is one of the most densely populated countries, with a population of 142 million and a density of 964 people per square kilometre (BBS, 2014). Despite this large population, the country has only 566 public healthcare facilities primarily designed to support disadvantaged populations (DGHS, 2020). Among these, 474 are primary care hospitals, while 67 and 25 are designated as

secondary and tertiary care hospitals, respectively (Fig. 1). 

Administratively, Bangladesh is divided into seven divisions, each with distinct urban and rural characteristics (BBS, 2014). Below the divisional level, the country is further subdivided into districts (second), upazilas (third), and unions (fourth), forming a multi-layered administrative structure. In this study, unions serve as the spatial unit of analysis. 

Bangladesh is currently undergoing significant demographic shifts, with older adults projected to grow substantially in the coming decades (United Nations, 2022). This aging population is distributed across both densely populated urban areas and more remote rural regions, each facing unique healthcare challenges. Urban centers, such as Dhaka, the capital city located in the center of the country, benefit from a higher concentration of healthcare facilities (Fig. 1). However, these areas also grapple with issues like overcrowding and increased demand for services. In contrast, rural regions often experience a scarcity of healthcare resources, forcing residents to travel long distances to access necessary care (Rahman et al., 2024). 

3.2. Data sources 

In evaluating healthcare accessibility across Bangladesh, this study uses a wide range of datasets, including the locations of multitier public healthcare services with bed count information, socio-demographic data, and road network data, as outlined in Table 1. The spatial unit of analysis is the union, the smallest administrative unit in Bangladesh for which population data is available (BBS, 2014). The centroids of the unions are used as origins in the accessibility analysis. 

The destinations are the locations of government-funded multi-tier public healthcare services. The healthcare system in Bangladesh is categorized into four levels: 1) community clinics, 2) primary, 3) secondary, and 4) tertiary care services (Zafri et al., 2021). At the community level, a community clinic offers the most basic healthcare services, typically staffed by a healthcare assistant and a family welfare assistant. Each clinic is designed to provide essential healthcare services, including maternal and child healthcare, with limited curative care (WHO, 2015). In contrast, primary-level healthcare services generally have a larger team, including nine doctors, a pharmacist, a radiographer, two medical assistants, and an Expanded Program on Immunization (EPI) technician (Mashreky et al., 2010). These facilities offer a broader range of services, such as inpatient and outpatient care, maternal and child healthcare, and disease control. Secondary healthcare services, serving at the district level, provide comprehensive curative and preventive care, outpatient and inpatient services, and health promotion, including theatre services for surgeries. The highest level of healthcare, the tertiary level, includes postgraduate medical institutes, specialized healthcare centers, medical college hospitals, and infectious disease hospitals (Islam et al., 2014). Given that community clinics in Bangladesh provide only basic services without doctors and bed capacity, this analysis excludes them, focusing on primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare systems. 

The enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) accessibility measure requires additional data inputs such as supply and demand information. Older people information, representing demand, and healthcare capacity (i.e., number of beds), representing supply, are obtained from the Population & Housing Census of Bangladesh (bbs.gov.bd) for 2011 and the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) for 2020, respectively, as detailed in Table 1. It is important to note that DGHS does not provide spatial location data for hospitals. To obtain this, we geocoded the healthcare facility locations using the addresses provided by DGHS (hospitaldghs.gov. bd/list-of-2ndary-tertiary-level-hospital/), cross-referencing with Google Maps. Additionally, we created network datasets utilizing road network data for the 2014 to 2020 period obtained from the Population & Housing Census of Bangladesh (Table 1). 

In this study, we define older adults as individuals aged 60 years or older. While there is no consensus on the age threshold for defining older adults in Bangladesh, we adopt the definition provided by the World Bank (2023) for older adults in Bangladesh as those who are 60 years or older. 

4. Methods 

4.1. Healthcare accessibility analysis 

In this study, we assess spatial healthcare accessibility using the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method introduced by Luo and Qi (2009). Crucially, two context-specific decisions must be made to operationalize this method effectively: the selection of appropriate travel modes that reflect real-world travel behaviour, and the determination of travel time thresholds to define catchment areas around healthcare facilities. Both choices have significant implications for how accessibility is measured and interpreted, particularly in a setting like Bangladesh, where transportation infrastructure is uneven and informal modes dominate. 

The following two subsections describe how we address each of these components. Section 4.1.1 outlines our approach to selecting representative travel modes, while Section 4.1.2 explains how travel time thresholds are determined for each tier of healthcare service.

4.1.1. Travel mode selection 

To select travel modes that realistically reflect older adults’ access to healthcare in Bangladesh, we draw on empirical data on everyday travel behaviour. Specifically, we use household travel survey data from the Dhaka Transport Coordination Authority (DTCA), which identifies walking, rickshaws, CNG (compressed natural gas) auto-rickshaws, human haulers, and buses as the most commonly used modes of transport in the capital (Nakshi and Debnath, 2021; see Fig. 2 for illustrations). Due to a lack of comparable data from other regions, we assume these modes are also prevalent across the country, particularly in other urban and peri-urban areas. However, identifying frequently used modes is only the first step. Incorporating them into the E2SFCA framework requires a realistic understanding of Bangladesh’s transportation landscape. The country’s transport system is highly fragmented and varies significantly between urban and rural areas. Public transportation is often informal and lacks adequate rural coverage (Alam et al., 2016), while private car ownership is relatively low and concentrated among urban elites (Statista, 2024). Informal modes like rickshaws are widely used but are typically limited to short-distance travel (Hossain and Susilo, 2011). 

To account for these contextual realities, we adopt a mode-specific approach in our analysis. When measuring accessibility to primary healthcare services, which are typically delivered through community-based or local-level facilities, we use rickshaws (see Fig. 2) as the representative mode of travel. For secondary and tertiary healthcare, which tend to be located in larger population centers and require longer travel distances, we use CNG auto-rickshaws —functionally similar to taxis in the Bangladeshi context—as the primary mode. These mode-specific choices allow us to accurately capture actual travel behaviours and delineate realistic catchment areas within the E2SFCA model. 

4.1.2. Determining travel time thresholds 

After identifying appropriate travel modes, the next step in applying the E2SFCA method is to define travel time thresholds for each category of healthcare facility. These thresholds determine the size of each catchment area and directly affect how accessibility is quantified. Setting realistic and context-specific thresholds is therefore essential for producing meaningful and policy-relevant results. — To calculate travel time, we first estimate the average speeds for the selected modes of transportation rickshaws for primary healthcare, and CNG auto-rickshaws for secondary and tertiary healthcare. However, reliable speed data for these informal and semiformal modes is limited. To address this, we rely on estimates from a Dhaka-based study on public transport accessibility (Rahman et al., 2022), which report average travel speeds of 5.77 km/h for rickshaws and 9.28 km/h for CNG auto-rickshaws. 

Using these speed values, we calculate the travel time from the centroid of each union to the nearest primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare facility. This is done using the Network Analyst Tool in ArcGIS Pro (ArcGIS, 2023), with road network data obtained from the Bangladesh Bureau of Statistics (BBS, 2014). The Closest Facility Tool is used to identify the nearest relevant facility for each union. We adopt a nearest-facility approach based on established practices in the literature, which typically assess healthcare accessibility by considering only the closest available facility (Delamater et al., 2012; Mizen, 2015; Yang et al., 2006; Jordan et al.,

2004). We then average the travel time for each category of healthcare facility across all unions and use these values as travel time thresholds in the E2SFCA model. 

The average travel time across unions is 1.56 h for primary healthcare facilities (by rickshaw), 2.69 h for secondary healthcare, and 5.06 h for tertiary healthcare (both by CNG auto-rickshaw), as shown in Table 2. These values serve as empirically grounded thresholds for delineating spatial catchment areas in the subsequent accessibility analysis. Building on these thresholds, the following section outlines how we apply the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method to measure spatial accessibility to multi-tier healthcare services. 

4.1.3. Measuring accessibility using E2SFCA 

To assess spatial accessibility to healthcare, we apply the enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) method developed by Luo and Qi (2009). The E2SFCA approach delineates a provider’s service area based on predefined travel time or distance thresholds while incorporating provider capacity, population demand, and a distance decay function to reflect reduced accessibility with increasing distance. It builds on the original two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method introduced by Luo and Wang (2003), which calculated supply-to-demand ratios within fixed catchments but assumed uniform accessibility throughout. The enhanced version addresses this limitation by applying distance-based weights, allowing for a more realistic representation of spatial access. As a result, it has become a widely adopted tool in healthcare accessibility research, including applications to multi-tier hospital services (Cheng et al., 2020; Zhang and Wang, 2021; Li and Wang, 2022), emergency care (Shin and Lee, 2018), primary care (McGrail and Humphreys, 2009), and aged care (Liu et al., 2022; Guida et al., 2021). 

To implement the E2SFCA method, we rely on five key data components: the geographic locations of population centers (demand) and healthcare facilities (supply), the transport network connecting them, and attributes of both supply and demand. In our analysis, supply is measured by the number of hospital beds at each facility, and demand corresponds to the older adult population at the union level. We apply the mode-specific travel time thresholds derived in Section 4.1.2–1.56 h for primary healthcare (by rickshaw), and 2.69 and 5.06 h for secondary and tertiary healthcare, respectively (by CNG auto-rickshaw). 

The E2SFCA method proceeds in two main computational steps. In Step 1 , for each healthcare facility j , we identify all the population centers i (i.e., centroids of unions) located within a predefined travel time threshold t 0 from healthcare j . Using Equation (1), we calculate the supply-to-demand ratio Rj , where supply is defined as the bed capacity Gj of healthcare facility j , and demand is the number of older adults Pi within its catchment area, weighted by a distance decay function.

4.2. Inequality analysis 

The concept of equality focuses on examining whether resources, opportunities, and benefits from transport systems such as accessibility are equally distributed across regions or groups of people (Chen and Jia, 2019; Geurs and van Wee, 2004; Karner et al., 2024). 

A recent study by Karner et al. (2024) reviewed the methods and practices commonly used in the literature to evaluate transportation equity. This study evaluated and compared four widely used inequality measures: the Gini index, Theil’s index, the Palma ratio, and the Concentration index. The findings highlighted the limitations of the Gini and Theil indices, particularly their disregard for socioeconomic characteristics, which prevents them from identifying whether better- or worse-off groups benefit disproportionately. This study recommended the Palma ratio and Concentration index as more effective measures since they incorporate socioeconomic information. However, socioeconomic data, such as income information, is often unavailable in LMICs. 

Therefore, in this study, we employ the Gini index to explore inequalities in healthcare accessibility for older adults at the division level across Bangladesh. The Gini index offers a general perspective on inequality in resource distributions like the distribution of accessibility (Ahmed et al., 2025; Jang et al., 2017; Lee and Kim, 2023; Liu et al., 2022). This is widely used, effective, and easy to interpret (Cheng et al., 2020; Lucas et al., 2016). A Gini index value of 1 indicates perfect unequal distribution, while a value of 0 indicates complete equal distribution. 

5. Results 

5.1. Travel time to multi-tier healthcare services 

Fig. 3 provides an overview of the spatial distributions of travel times to primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare locations across Bangladesh. The outcomes of this analysis serve as the basis for determining the sizes of catchment areas for the E2SFCA analysis. The findings suggest that primary healthcare services are generally the most accessible, with average travel times typically within 2 h across the country (Fig. 3a). Most areas, both urban and rural, exhibit better availability of primary care services, with a total average travel time of 1.56 h to the nearest primary healthcare services. This 1.56 h is used as the size of the catchment area for the E2SFCA analysis of primary healthcare services. Although this distribution ensures that the majority portion of older adults can access basic healthcare services with minimal travel, travelling an average of 1.56 h for basic healthcare is neither ideal nor desirable. 

In contrast, secondary healthcare services are less evenly distributed across space (Fig. 3b). They are primarily concentrated in urban areas, with a total average travel time of 2.69 h to the nearest secondary healthcare services from unions, which is used as the travel time threshold for the E2SFCA analysis of secondary care services. In general, urban populations benefit from shorter travel times, while rural areas, particularly in the west, south, southeastern, and northeastern regions, face longer travel times.

Tertiary healthcare services exhibit the longest travel times, often exceeding 5.06 h (i.e., a travel time threshold for the E2SFCA analysis of tertiary care services) (Fig. 3c). These services are predominantly located in major cities, especially near the capital city Dhaka in the center of Bangladesh, resulting in light green areas covering much of the urban landscape (Fig. 3c). In contrast, the older population in remote and less developed regions must travel considerable times (shown in deep blue) to access advanced medical treatments and emergency care (Fig. 3c). 

Comparing these three levels of healthcare services highlights significant disparities in the spatial distribution of multi-tier healthcare locations. While primary healthcare is relatively accessible nationwide, secondary and tertiary services are heavily concentrated in urban areas. This urban-rural divide presents challenges for older adults in rural populations in accessing higher-level healthcare. 

At the division level, travel time to healthcare in Bangladesh varies significantly, with notable differences in travel times to primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare facilities (Table 3). Dhaka has the shortest average travel time to the nearest primary healthcare facility at 1.42 h, while Sylhet and Khulna have the longest at 1.74 h. For secondary healthcare, Khulna has the shortest travel time at 2.25 h, whereas Barisal has the longest at 3.23 h. The greatest variation is observed in travel time to tertiary healthcare, where Dhaka has the shortest time at 4.15 h, while Khulna has the longest at 6.91 h. 

5.2. Accessibility to healthcare services 

Fig. 4 illustrates the geographic distribution of E2SFCA-based accessibility to primary (Fig. 4a), secondary (Fig. 4b), and tertiary (Fig. 4c) healthcare services across Bangladesh. While direct comparisons across healthcare types are not appropriate due to typespecific normalization, the maps reveal notable differences in spatial coverage and distribution patterns for each service tier. 

Primary healthcare services (Fig. 4a) display the widest spatial coverage, with accessible locations relatively well-dispersed across most regions of the country, including many rural areas. Secondary healthcare services (Fig. 4b), however, show more spatial clustering, with accessibility concentrated around urban centers. Older adults in the northwest, southwest, and central regions benefit from relatively better access, while remote areas, particularly in the southern (coastal) and southeastern (hilly) parts of the country, experience widespread accessibility gaps. 

Tertiary healthcare services (Fig. 4c) demonstrate the most limited geographic coverage, with accessibility largely confined to major cities. A substantial share of remote and rural areas, including the Southeastern (Chattogram division), South and Southwestern (Barisal and Khulna divisions), Northeast (Sylhet division), and Northwest (Rajshahi division), lacks access to tertiary care. Even within the Dhaka division, where many tertiary facilities are concentrated, most areas fall into the very low to moderate accessibility categories. 

Table 4 presents the average healthcare accessibility scores at the division level based on the normalized enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) accessibility measures. Primary healthcare accessibility is highest in Chattogram (0.00140), while Dhaka has the lowest accessibility score (0.00062). For secondary healthcare accessibility, Sylhet has the highest score — (0.03394), whereas Rajshahi has the lowest (0.01838). In terms of tertiary care, Dhaka despite its central location and relatively advanced urban infrastructure—has a modest accessibility score of 0.05848, only marginally higher than other divisions. Khulna records the lowest accessibility to tertiary care (0.01772), indicating severe limitations in specialized healthcare services. 

Based on these findings, Fig. 5 delineates the overlapped multi-tier healthcare access zones, categorizing them into lacking service zones (no healthcare services available), limited service zones (only one tier of healthcare services available), effective service zones (two tiers of healthcare services available), and core service zones (all tiers of healthcare services available) (Cheng et al., 2020). The core service zones, which include all tiers of healthcare services, are scattered and primarily surround major cities such as Dhaka, Chattogram, Sylhet, Rangpur, and Khulna. Effective service zones, where two tiers of healthcare services are available, cover most parts of Bangladesh, predominantly in the Dhaka, Rangpur, Rajshahi, and Khulna divisions (Fig. 5). In contrast, both limited and lacking service zones are widely distributed and mostly located in rural and remote areas, particularly in the southeastern regions, with a significant concentration in the Chattogram and Sylhet divisions. These healthcare access shortage areas, highlighted in orange and red in Fig. 5, indicate zones with limited or no access to healthcare services, marking them as priorities for policy interventions.

5.3. Inequality in spatial accessibility 

Fig. 6 illustrates the Gini coefficients for primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare across the divisions of Bangladesh, highlighting the degree of inequality in healthcare accessibility distribution within each division. Across all divisions, the Gini coefficients tend to increase with the level of healthcare, indicating greater inequality in access to higher-tier services. For instance, tertiary healthcare consistently exhibits the highest Gini values (except for Dhaka and Chattogram), suggesting that access to specialized tertiary healthcare is more uneven than access to primary or secondary care. 

To better understand divisions with inadequate healthcare accessibility and inequality, Fig. 7 plots min-max normalized (for each healthcare type) average accessibility scores (from Table 4) across divisions (Cheng et al., 2020) on the X-axis against Gini coefficients on the Y-axis. This provides a straightforward understanding of regions requiring urgent policy interventions to improve accessibility and reduce disparities. 

In primary care (Fig. 7a), five out of seven divisions— Dhaka, Khulna, Rangpur, Sylhet, and Rajshahi—fall into the high – inequality low accessibility quadrant, highlighting critical accessibility gaps. Chattogram and Barisal also exhibit high inequality but have comparatively better accessibility. For secondary care (Fig. 7b), Dhaka and Rajshahi remain in the high inequality–low accessibility quadrant. Khulna, previously classified in this category, has shifted to the higher accessibility category but continues to experience significant inequality, with a Gini coefficient exceeding 0.8. In tertiary care (Fig. 7c), the overall scenario improves, with – only Khulna and Chattogram remaining in the high inequality low accessibility quadrant. Rajshahi, Sylhet, and Rangpur cluster near moderate accessibility but still suffer from substantial inequality, while Dhaka exhibits the highest accessibility but remains above a Gini coefficient of 0.8, indicating a high level of accessibility inequality. 

– Across all tiers, no division falls into the low inequality high accessibility quadrant, confirming that healthcare access remains both highly unequal and insufficient nationwide. The findings highlight the urgent need for targeted policy interventions, particularly in regions, where disparities and low accessibility are most pronounced across all tiers of healthcare. 

6. Discussion and conclusion 

In response to the demographic shift towards an aging population in Bangladesh, ensuring equitable and adequate healthcare

access for older adults has become an increasingly urgent concern. With older adults requiring more frequent and specialized care due to declining health, the demand for healthcare services is expected to reach unprecedented levels in Bangladesh. As such, preparing the healthcare and transport systems to meet these needs should become a priority. A crucial first step towards this goal is to assess the current state of healthcare and transport infrastructures by evaluating spatial accessibility to healthcare services. This study focuses 

11 

N. Ahmed et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                        Journal of Transport & Health 44 (2025) 102121 

==> picture [428 x 274] <==

Fig. 6. The Gini coefficients of multi-tier healthcare accessibility within divisions in Bangladesh. 

specifically on the spatial accessibility of public healthcare services across Bangladesh, aiming to uncover disparities in access to multitier healthcare services among older adults at the national level. 

The spatial distribution of healthcare accessibility in Bangladesh highlights uneven patterns and accessibility gaps. While direct comparisons across healthcare types are not appropriate due to type-specific normalization, differences in spatial coverage and distribution are evident. Primary healthcare services exhibit broad geographic coverage, with accessible facilities more widely distributed across both urban and rural regions. In contrast, secondary healthcare services show a more uneven distribution, with many regions, particularly in divisions like Barisal and Chattogram, experiencing limited accessibility within the travel time threshold. Tertiary healthcare services demonstrate the most constrained spatial coverage, with accessibility largely concentrated in major urban centers such as Dhaka. This urban concentration leads to substantial service gaps for older adults residing in rural and remote divisions, including Khulna and parts of Chattogram. These findings are consistent with prior studies, such as Cheng et al. (2020), which observed declining accessibility for older adults at higher tiers of healthcare. Similarly, limited healthcare access in rural and remote regions has been noted by McGrail and Humphreys (2014a,b) and Shah et al. (2017). The delineation of multi-tier healthcare access zones (Fig. 5) underscores the concentration of services in urban hubs in contrast to widespread service deficits in peripheral and southeastern regions. Inequality assessments using Gini coefficients highlight disparities in healthcare accessibility, particularly severe in tertiary care across divisions, indicating critical areas demanding targeted policy interventions to mitigate inequities and enhance healthcare provision for older adults nationwide. 

The findings also highlight the critical role of transportation in healthcare accessibility, particularly for older adults who face substantial travel burdens in Bangladesh. Long travel times (see Table 2) for primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare highlight severe inequities in access compared to other countries, such as China, where travel times are significantly shorter (Cheng et al., 2020). These prolonged travel durations may deter older adults from seeking timely medical care, exacerbating health disparities and leading to worsened health outcomes (Kelly et al., 2016). The reliance on informal transport modes, such as rickshaws and human haulers, further complicates access, as these options are often unavailable or impractical for long-distance travel (Hossain and Susilo, 2011). Addressing these challenges requires targeted policy interventions, including investments in age-friendly public transport (Tinella et al., 2023), improved paratransit services (Miah et al., 2020), and better integration of healthcare planning with transportation networks (Marsden et al., 2007). Expanding affordable and reliable transport options, such as subsidized shuttle services to healthcare facilities, could significantly reduce travel burdens (Guo et al., 2022). Additionally, decentralizing healthcare services by expanding secondary and tertiary care facilities in underserved areas could help bridge accessibility gaps (Rahman et al., 2024). Without such measures, disparities in healthcare access will persist, disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations, particularly older adults in rural and peri-urban regions. 

From a policy perspective, this study contributes to addressing critical healthcare challenges outlined by the World Health Organization (2015), particularly issues related to inadequate service coverage and inequitable accessibility. While accessibility

Fig. 7. Inequality in accessibility to multi-tier healthcare services: a) primary, b) secondary, and c) tertiary. 

disparities are not unique to Bangladesh among LMICs, the extent, spatial patterns, and underlying causes of these inequalities reflect distinct contextual realities. One key factor that sets Bangladesh apart is the type of transportation commonly used by older populations. In countries like China, older adults may access healthcare using public transit or private vehicles. In contrast, many in Bangladesh rely on non-motorized or semi-motorized modes such as rickshaws and CNG auto-rickshaws. These modes are typically slower, more expensive for low-income older adults, and often involve longer travel due to fragmented transport infrastructure, such as poor road connectivity, as well as uneven distribution of health facilities and limited availability of nearby services. By explicitly incorporating these locally common transport modes into the analysis, this study offers a context-specific and policy-relevant assessment of healthcare accessibility. It fills a critical gap in the literature and provides practical guidance for more equitable healthcare planning in Bangladesh. 

This study has several limitations. Firstly, the analysis relies on outdated census datasets from 2011, reflecting challenges in 

13 

N. Ahmed et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                        Journal of Transport & Health 44 (2025) 102121 

acquiring and using up-to-date data in the LMIC context, particularly in the Global South (Ahmed et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2023). Secondly, the accessibility analysis employs a travel time-based threshold derived from averaging travel time to the nearest healthcare services. A defined threshold set by the local government could offer a more intuitive perspective. Thirdly, the road network used for accessibility analysis has limited road connectivity in hilly and remote areas, as a result, we had to exclude some parts from the analysis. Fourthly, geocoding of healthcare locations was performed using Google Maps due to insufficient detailed address information from the current provider, potentially affecting accuracy. 

Despite its limitations, this study contributes to the existing literature in several important ways. It is among the first to comprehensively assess healthcare accessibility across Bangladesh, with a particular focus on the aging population. The analysis maps the spatial distribution of primary, secondary, and tertiary care by calculating travel times and identifying areas with limited access across the country. It further evaluates spatial accessibility to multi-tier healthcare services for older adults by incorporating supply factors (hospital capacity), demand (older adult population), distance decay, and locally prevalent transport modes. In addition, the study highlights disparities in accessibility using a well-established inequality index. These findings support local policy-making by identifying underserved regions where older adults face significant barriers to care. Such evidence-based insights are essential for optimizing the allocation of healthcare resources, particularly for aging populations. In contexts like Bangladesh, where financial resources are constrained, expanding healthcare facilities without strategic planning may not only fail to improve access but may also result in inefficient use of public funds. This study, therefore, provides a critical foundation for targeted, equitable, and cost-effective policy interventions. 

In conclusion, this study represents a pioneering effort to investigate disparities in geographic accessibility to multi-tier public healthcare services for the older population in an understudied LMIC, Bangladesh. By highlighting the challenges associated with accessing these healthcare services in Bangladesh, our research contributes to the broader understanding of healthcare accessibility in response to the rapidly aging societies of the Global South.

1. Introduction 

Breast cancer continues to be the most common form of cancers diagnosed among Iowan women and the rate of new cases of breast cancer has been increasing since 2013 (West et al., 2023). Iowa ranks in the top 10 in breast cancer incidence with the fourth highest increasing trend (National Cancer Institute, 2024). Through mammography screening, more cancers can be found at localized (early) stage, where 5-year survival rates are nearly 100 %” (West et al., 2023). Early detection through mammograms has been shown to reduce breast cancer mortality by as much as 40–67 % (Giaquinto et al., 2022; Tab´ar et al., 2019). However, not all women utilize these lifesaving screenings equally. Later stage of diagnosis and poorer breast cancer survival has disproportionately impacted individuals of lower socioeconomic status and those who reside in rural areas (Nattinger et al., 2017; Moubadder et al., 2022; Chandak et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2021). Lack of participation in mammography screening has been linked to lower access to health care, lack of transportation, and other social determinants such as lower income and unemployment (Abdel-Aleem et al., 2016; Guillaume et al., 2017; Spak et al., 2022; DeSantis et al., 2017). Rural and minority population inequities are well-documented, with geographic disparities 

in health care availability and accessibility contributing significantly to the uneven breast cancer diagnosis stage and mortality outcomes. (Moubadder et al., 2022)[,] (Webster et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2021; Rosenkrantz et al., 2017; Coughlin et al., 2002)[–] (Webster et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2021; Rosenkrantz et al., 2017; Coughlin et al., 2002)These have emphasized the role of place in understanding the barriers to rural patients such as provider shortages, longer travel times, and limited-service capacity. 

While geographic availability and accessibility of mammogram screening facilities are foundational aspects of overall potential access and utilization of breast cancer screening, the ability to receive a mammography screening also depends upon the capacity of facilities to serve patients (Penchansky and Thomas, 1981). Acquiring health care services requires that a clinic must be open, staffed, equipped, and have appointments available to serve patients. Spatial accessibility considers both geographic accessibility and appointment availability simultaneously. Optimization models have been developed to examine both availability (location of the health care facilities), accessibility (the driving distance and time along the road network between the patient residence and the facilities), and capacity (is the facility able to provide services to all the patients within the travel threshold) (Hodgart, 1978;

> C.S. Wiener et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                               Health and Place 94 (2025) 103488

Zheng et al., 2018; Young et al., 2020a, 2020b). While optimization models help to evaluate potential access to mammography screening services, utilization is the realized action of receiving a mammogram and may take place in different ways than predicted in an access model. 

Spatial accessibility, insurance coverage, and rurality have all been linked to adherence to mammography screening recommendations (Gentil et al., 2012; Gutnik and Castro; Jazieh and Soora, 2001). Previous studies have explored spatial accessibility to cancer screening among Canadian provinces (Pannu et al., 2022; St-Jacques et al., 2013), France (Prajapati et al., 2022), and other US states including Arkansas (Young et al., 2020b), the Lower Mississippi Delta region (Zahnd et al., 2019), and Delaware (Webster et al., 2023), however the optimization method has never been applied to Iowa while using actual capacity of each facility that offers mammograms (Dai, 2010). In the passing of the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (ACA), recommended preventative screening services, such as annual mammograms, are required to be offered at no cost by private insurance and Medicare for average risk women in alignment with United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommendations.[30][–][32] As a result, most patients are likely to utilize their health insurance when receiving a mammogram since it will result in no cost sharing (Alharbi et al., 2019; Eguia et al., 2018). Private insurance claims data thus capture the majority of individuals receiving mammograms and are useful to understanding the realized access and driving distance patterns among the women receiving annual screening. Analyzing the realized utilization patterns of patients seeking mammograms can reveal some of the strengths and/or limits of the theoretical access models. 

This study had two objectives. The first objective was to map the supply and theoretical demand for mammography screening services in Iowa from 2016 to 2022 to identify areas where demand is unmet due to inadequate spatial access. We evaluated the extent to which the number of facilities and mammography machines were able to meet the potential demand for screening, based upon the population of women of recommended screening age, over the study period. We also determined the areas of Iowa that have the greatest unmet demand due to poor spatial accessibility and whether these areas changed over time from 2016 to 2022. 

The second objective was to understand the relationship between driving distance and utilization among those women of recommended age for mammography screening who have private insurance from a large provider of health insurance in Iowa. We investigated the utilization rates for annual mammography screening of women within recommended annual screening age each year from 2017 to 2022. We evaluated the driving distances that women drove to receive their mammograms to identify areas where spatial barriers and travel burdens are the highest. Lastly, we explored if those areas with low access in the models also are areas where women driving long distances to have a mammogram. 

2. Data and methods 

2.1. Spatial accessibility 

The address and number of machines per mammography facility for 2016–2022 were obtained from The Iowa Department of Health and Human Services’ (IHHS) Bureau of Radiological Health. The IHHS is an agency approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to provide certification to mammogram facilities. These facilities must be certified by the IHHS and must maintain continued accreditation with annual inspections in order to provide mammography services. 

From the IHHS mammography facility data, mobile clinics were excluded (total n = 25, range each year n = 2–5), as has been done in other studies, because they are assumed to not be universally available and accessible (Young et al., 2020b; Onega et al., 2014). Federal facilities, including Veteran’s Administration, military bases, correctional facilities, and Indian Health Service hospitals and clinics were not 

included in the database as they are not under the jurisdiction of the IHHS and they have restrictions regarding who can access the services. 

The remaining facilities with their number of mammography machines were geocoded to the street address using ArcGIS Pro 10.8.2 (Esri, Redlands, CA). The capacity of each machine was estimated using the 2006 Government Accountability Office definition of maximum capacity as three mammograms per machine per business hour (Young et al., 2020b; United States Government Accountability Office, 2006). We further approximated that 75 % of mammograms performed at these facilities were screening mammograms rather than diagnostic mammograms (Young et al., 2020b; L. K et al., 2012). This resulted in an estimation of 4500 screening mammograms per machine each year. 

Demographic data for the adult female population of Iowa were obtained from the 2016–2022 American Community Surveys (ACS) of the US Census Bureau. This annual survey data provides population estimates in 5-year groupings by sex at the ZIP code Tabulation Area (ZCTA) level. ZCTAs are the unit of analysis as they change less frequently than zip code areas, allowing for more stability of areal units across years. Demand estimates for women of recommended screening age were generated for females aged 40 to 84 for each ZCTA in Iowa for – each year 2016 2022. While there is variability in the recommendation of timing of screening (annual or biannual) and the starting and ending ages of screening mammograms, studies have found that annual screening mammography of women aged 40 to 84 prevents more deaths – from breast cancer than biennial screening of women 50 74 years old (Niell et al., 2017). 

To measure potential spatial accessibility to mammogram screening from 2016 to 2022, we used an optimization model that incorporates both travel time and capacity constraints (Young et al., 2020a, 2020b). In each year, the optimization model allocates the demand (number of annual screenings required based on population of women aged 40–84) to the supply (number of mammograms available that year at each ZCTA) within a 30-min driving time from the demand centroid. Drive time estimates were based upon an origin-destination cost matrix for each year that determines the shortest road travel times between each ZCTA centroid and ranks them according to their driving time until the time threshold is met. This created the order of choice for allocation of the population demand from ZCTA centroid to the nearest ZCTA centroid with mammography screening capacity. 

Each model starts by selecting one ZCTA centroid at random and allocating its population to the nearest centroid with mammography capacity until the machine capacity of that ZCTA is reached or the entire – population of women aged 40 84 of the ZCTA is allocated. Any unallocated population can be allocated to the next nearest ZCTA with available capacity within the maximum 30-min driving time threshold. Once all the demand has been allocated or all the available capacity within the 30-min driving time threshold has been exhausted, another ZCTA centroid with demand is selected at random. The process repeats until no further allocation can take place or all capacity is used. 

Since the order in which the ZCTA centroids are selected may change the allocation results, 1000 random simulations were run, and the results were summarized. We classified the ZCTAs where the population could never be completely allocated to a facility with capacity within 30 min of driving time across all 1000 permutations as “No Access”. We report the mean unallocated population for each ZCTA across all scenarios, the proportion of scenarios that were unable to fully allocate the population, as well as the proportion of total Iowa population of women able to be allocated for each simulation. 

– Rurality was evaluated using rural urban commuting area (RUCA) codes, the most recent codes are based upon the 2010 US Census, which were assigned to each ZCTA. We consolidated from the 10 initial RUCA codes down to 5 levels of increasing rurality following heme 3 of the Washington State Department of Health Guidelines that uses Urban Core, Suburban, Large Rural Town, Small Town, and Rural. (Guidelines for Using Rural) heme 3 separates small towns from rural areas to account for fewer neighbors and farther travel distances to resources.

2.2. Utilization of screening 

In the second portion of this study, we evaluated utilization of mammography screening in Iowa. While urban and higher income individuals are more likely to enroll in private health insurance than rural and lower-income individuals (Terlizzi and Cohen, 2020). We used 2016–2022 enrollment and claims data from a commercial health insurer for women residing in Iowa who were aged 40 to 64 (from lowest in 2020 at n = 188,412 to highest in 2017 at n = 211,375). This age range reflected the age at which the insurer begins to cover screening mammography (age 40), while excluding those aged 65 and older who would be eligible for Medicare. Data for this study was from a deidentified, commercial insurance data repository securely housed at the University of Iowa College of Public Health. The study and use of the data repository is conducted under Institution Review Board (number 200811728). 

Utilization rates were determined for each ZCTA by evaluating the – number of women aged 40 64 who received a screening mammogram – that year versus the number of women aged 40 64 who are privately insured in that ZCTA each year. The number of women who had insurance were determined through claims data. All women aged 40–64 were included, however, records of patients who were younger than 40 or older than 64 at the time of mammogram receipt, were excluded from the study population. If no mammogram was received within that year, then age was calculated based upon birth month and birth year compared to the month and year the data was pulled. If age was less than 40 or greater than 65, the record was excluded. 

Claims were then evaluated by the Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) code. Only those claims that included the CPT code for a screening mammogram were included. The codes that applied to diagnostic and screening mammograms changed annually from 2016 to 2019; we thus used the year-specific codes to include any codes that were for screening mammograms and exclude any mammograms or imaging that were classified as diagnostic. 

We excluded duplicate claims for patient ID, date of service, type of service, and provider ZCTA. Any woman who received a diagnostic mammogram unless they received a screening mammogram in that same year (range from n = 158 in 2020 to n = 289 in 2016). 

Each record was assigned to the ZCTA that is associated with the Zip Code of the patient’s residence. Each woman only had one eligibility record per ZCTA location per year. The number of claims for screening mammograms per eligible population for each ZCTA determined the utilization rates for each ZCTA. 

To understand how driving distance impacted utilization, we evaluated the proportion of claims for each ZCTA that drove over 30 min or over 60 min from their residence to the facility ZCTA that they received their mammogram. The driving times were determined using the origindestination cost matrix created in the Network Analysis tool of ArcGIS Pro 10.8.2 (Esri, Redlands, CA). This creates a list of driving time estimates based upon the shortest road travel times between each ZCTA centroid of patient residence to the other ZCTA centroids of providers. 

The count of mammogram screening claims was assigned to each origin-destination pair and categorized as driving time within 30 min (or 60 min) or above 30 or 60 min. The total number of claims from the origin ZCTA were calculated and the number of claims within and over 30 min (or 60 min) driving time were assigned to that origin ZCTA. The relevance index was calculated as proportion of the claims from the origin ZCTA greater than 30 min and 60 min driving times compared to the total number of claims from the origin ZCTA. This metric identifies areas of Iowa with less accessibility to mammography screening based 

upon utilization patterns of patents living in these areas. 

We examined the patterns of utilization rates and relevance index by rurality and age groups. Using the American Community Survey 5-year age group population estimates of women, we calculated the percentage of women enrolled in the private insurance for each age category: 40–44, 45–49, 50–54, 55–59, and 60–64. We then evaluated the percentages of utilization of mammography screening services by the same age groups. 

We evaluated rurality in a similar fashion to that of spatial accessibility, using the same 5-category breakdown of the RUCA codes based upon the 2010 census. Using the percentage of total population, we evaluated the utilization rates for each category: Urban Core, Suburban, Large Rural Town, Small Town, and Rural. We also calculated the percentage of each rurality category where patients drove more than 30 min and 60 min to a mammography screening. 

3. Results 

3.1. Spatial accessibility 

Table 1 shows the state population demand for women of recommended mammography screening age rose from 1,070,254 in 2016 to 1,101,926 in 2022. Approximately 540,000 (2016) to 584,000 (2022) women aged 40–84 years (50.8 %–53.0 %) live in Urban core areas, while 94,000 to 95,000 (8.8 %) are living in Suburban areas. A – decreasing number of women aged 40 84 years are living in large rural towns, small towns, and rural areas. Approximately 163,000 (15.3 %) women living in large rural towns in 2016 dropped to 160,000 (14.5 %) in 2022. Small towns and rural areas together make up about 268,000 women (25 %) in 2016 which decreased to 261,000 (23.7 %) in 2022. Although small percent changes, demand increased slightly in urban areas throughout the study period while demand decreased slightly in these large rural towns and rural areas. 

The total estimated capacity of mammography screening was at its lowest in 2017 with 167 mammogram machines and an estimated capacity of 751,500 screenings, rising to the highest estimated capacity of 796,500 in 2022 with 177 mammogram machines (Fig. 1). Every year the demand for mammography screening based upon annual screening outweighed the capacity of the mammogram machines available in Iowa to meet the need of the requested mammograms. 

The number of ZCTAs that had at least one mammogram machine ranged from 117 in 2016 and 2017 to 122 in 2021. The distribution of mammogram machine availability can be found in Appendix Figure 1. The changes in the number of machines do not account for an improvement in accessibility over the years. In 2022, there were 177 mammogram machines in only 118 ZCTAs, some machine increases occurred in ZCTAs where other machines already were present. Seen in Fig. 1, most of the mammography screening facility capacity (38.8 % in 2016–36.7 % in 2022) is in Urban core areas, although Urban contribution to the total capacity is decreasing slightly from 2016 to 2022. Small Towns contribute an additional 30.2 %–31.1 % of total capacity across the years. Large Rural Towns contribute an increasing amount of capacity to the total (14.7 %–15.3 %). 

The spatial accessibility models were able to fully allocate the state population in approximately 87 % of the simulations (Fig. 2). In 2016, the mean allocation was approximately 88.5 % while in 2020, the year with the least ability to fully allocate demand was approximately 85.75 %. The data demonstrated consistent unallocated demand in various sections of the Eastern portion of Iowa, the far north along the Minnesota border, and the far west along the Nebraska border, however, there is variation from year to year (Fig. 3). Small towns and rural areas in central Iowa have improved access to mammography screening over the study period. The northern border of the state and the Eastern portion of the state consistently had poor spatial accessibility (less than 40 % of simulations able to fully allocate the ZCTA’s population) throughout the study period.

Suburban areas account for about 9 % of the population in Iowa from 2016 to 2022 (Table 1), however, they make up anywhere from 17.2 % in 2020 to 43.1 % of the unallocated population in 2022 (Fig. 4, Appendix A: Table A2). Rural Areas make up about 10 % of the total population but in 2016, 2018, 2021 accounted for about 17.5 % of the unallocated population and in 2022 accounted for 30.4 %. 3.2. Utilization of screening 

In the second portion of this study, utilization of mammography – screening by privately insured women 40 64 were evaluated by rurality and age. The utilization rates vary across the categories of rurality over the study period (Fig. 5). Urban Core (range: 41.0–46.4 %; mean: 44.3 – %) and Suburban (range: 40.2 45.3 %; mean: 43.2 %) categories have the highest utilization rates. Large Rural Towns (range: 37.2–42.9 %; mean: 40.8 %) and Small Towns (range: 38.1–43.3 %; mean: 40.8 %) have similar utilization rates lower than urban and suburban. Rural (range: 35.3–40.7 %; mean: 38.0 %) areas have the lowest utilization rates each year. 

Rates of utilization for privately insured women aged 40 to 64 in Iowa by age category indicate that the lowest utilization is among the youngest ages and highest among the oldest ages (Fig. 6). The utilization rates for all of Iowa, as defined as the number of women who were recommended to receive a screening mammogram in that year who were privately insured, range between the lowest rate in 2020 at 38.7 % and the highest rate at 44.1 % in 2022. For all seven years, the utilization percentages for women aged 40–44 and 45–49 were below the state utilization rate. The utilization rates for mammography screening across 

the study period is consistently lowest among women aged 40–44 (range: 29.0–34.3 %; mean: 31.5 %) and 45–49 (range: 36.5–43.2 %; mean: 39.5 %). For all seven years, the utilization percentages for women aged 50–54, 55–59, and 60–64 were consistently above the state utilization rate. Rates of mammography screening for women aged 50–54 range from 40.8 to 45.6 % (mean: 43.7 %) and for women aged 55–59 range from 41.8 to 47.7 % (mean: 45.6 %). The highest rates of mammography screening for privately insured women are among women aged 60–64 (range: 43.3–51.5 %; mean: 48.2 %). Utilization gradually increased from 2016 to 2019 for age groups 55–59 and – especially 60 64 but dipped in 2020. Since 2020, the utilization rates for screening mammography have been increasing steadily among all age groups. 

Patients did not travel the same distances to screening mammogram facilities. Fig. 7 shows that higher proportions of women aged 40–64 drove more than 30 min to receive a mammogram live in rural areas and small towns, especially those in the southern portions of the state, the northern border with Minnesota, and the rural areas in west-central and east-central Iowa. These patterns remained reasonably consistent from year to year. In all years, the highest proportions of claims for women aged 40–64 who received drove more than 60 min to receive their mammogram were in the west central portion of Iowa and the southern border along Missouri (Fig. 8). The west central area remained consistent from year to year with high proportions of claims for women having driven more than 1 h to their mammogram. The southern portion of the state had some variation year to year. 

To describe the discontinuity more clearly between the access model and the utilization, we took one year, 2019, of our study and compared the results of the access model and the drive time utilization results in Fig. 9. This map depicts the areas where the access model over or underestimated access compared to the proportion of women who drove over 30 min to receive their mammogram. We see that the urban areas of Des Moines and the east and west borders as well as the central north border have areas of underestimating access, meaning the model shows

4. Discussion 

The optimization model shows that with the given landscape of mammography screening services and machine capacities, which are likely overestimated, Iowa would not be able to achieve the utilization goals of the recommended population to receive annual mammogram screening. The model was only able to allocate on average approximately 86.7 % of the population. The model never allocated the entire demand population of Iowa to the available mammogram facilities within 30 min of patients. However, this allocation rate is higher than that of a similar study that found only about 60 % of the theoretical demand could be allocated to mammogram facilities in Arkansas (Young et al., 2020b). 

– The data portrays that the Healthy Iowans 2017 2021 goal of 85 % – of utilization of mammograms among women aged 50 74 by 2022 was 

possible with the given landscape of mammography screening services (West et al., 2022). However, surpassing this goal would be difficult as the number of ZCTAs that have mammogram facilities are decreasing despite the number of machines increasing slightly. The models shows that Iowa could never have 100 % of the population meeting the recommendations for screening age and frequency: given driving barriers for patients, the infrastructure and location of facilities, and staffing limits to facility capacity, full allocation could not currently be realized. 

The optimization model of spatial accessibility indicates suburban and rural areas are most at risk of not having access to mammography screening services. Suburban areas only have 6 machines (5 in 2022) making up only 3.5 % of the capacity of the state’s mammogram capacity, however the suburban areas account for 8.8 % of Iowa’s population of women of recommended screening age and anywhere from 17.2 % to 43.1 % of the populations that could not be allocated by the model. In the model, the suburban and urban population are often competing for the same mammogram machine capacity, meaning it is not only the suburban population that is competing for the 6 machines residing in the suburban ZCTAs. Depending on the order that the ZCTA’ are chosen for allocation, these machines’ capacity may have been met by the urban population nearby before the suburban population is allocated. Suburban areas have accessibility issues likely due to an underlying population demand concern, while rural areas have a travel distance concern limiting their accessibility. Suburban areas may be experiencing more complex concerns beyond driving distance to

The large rural town population of Iowa is also at risk of not having access to mammography screening. While the rural population makes up – only approximately 10 11 % of the state population and rural areas account for approximately 21–25 % of Iowa’ mammography machine capacity, there remain issues with spatial accessibility for these populations in our model. A large portion of Iowa is categorized as rural. While there are more machines in this category of rurality, the distance between these facilities and the population they serve is quite large. Our optimization model shows that rural populations make up between 8.6 and 30.4 % of the populations that cannot be allocated to a mammography machine within 30 min of their resident ZCTA. 

In the second part of our study, we examine the spatial accessibility models to the utilization and driving distance maps, we see differing patterns. This relationship between the theoretical accessibility to mammography screening and utilization of mammography screening 

shows that there is more to the decision making of patients to utilize a service than what we can measure. There is an uneven distribution of – patients aged 40 64 driving more than 30 min and 60 min to reach their mammogram facilities (Figs. 6–9). These patterns where large portion of patients drove farther to their mammogram screening remained stable across the study period. This indicates that there might be distance barriers that hold constant. These could be due to an overestimation of mammography machine capacity in these areas or shorter clinic operation hours in the areas surrounding hindering the number of mammograms available to be performed (Coughlin et al., 2002; Heller et al., 2018). 

Comparing access and utilization visually, the same areas where patients drove longer distances were not reflected as areas lacking access in the spatial accessibility models. The optimization model shows that there is access, but when we define the driving distance patterns of these patients, it indicates layers of complexity beyond driving distance and capacity that is not captured by our study. There are additional decisions being made by patients independent of driving distance, such as

As we saw in Fig. 9, there was some overlap between poor spatial accessibility seen in the potential access model and the women driving farther than 30 min for their mammograms. The access model was able to pick up some of the areas where driving distance is a barrier, however in several portions of the state, the model either over or under identified spatial accessibility concerns, compared to driving distance women drove to receive screening mammograms. These optimization models are able to capture the spatial barriers that may be having a larger impact on patients receiving their mammograms, however, they are imperfect when other decisions making behaviors are influencing the patterns of patients. 

The estimates of annual capacity for mammograms may also be greatly overestimating the capabilities of the facility to provide 4500 screening mammograms a year per machine. This may explain that we observe in central-west and south-central areas of Iowa where large proportions of the claims were for patients who drove over 60 min to their mammogram, but the access models indicate high local spatial accessibility since mammogram machines exist in these areas. We also know that patients may not utilize services near their residence, so measuring the driving distance between a patient’s residence and the location of her screening mammogram may be overestimating driving distance (McLafferty, 2003; Nemet and Bailey, 2000). 

Despite all the women in our utilization study having private insurance, their utilization rate for screening mammograms (41.8 %) is lower than the utilization rates reported in the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS), a survey which reported that the utilization rates for insured Iowan women was 58.7 % for women age 40–49 and 73.9 % for women age 50–74 (Miller et al., 2024a). While these low utilization rates may indicate overestimation of the self-reporting of the BRFSS survey, they also indicate that there are additional complexities 

beyond having spatial access and health insurance coverage that is driving the low rate of receiving a screening mammogram, such as provider recommendation patterns (Haas et al., 2017) or patients utilizing out-of-network or out-of-state providers. 

During this study period, the USPSTF which sets the preventive services recommendations that are required to be covered by private insurers without cost sharing under the ACA, recommended that all women receive biennial mammograms starting at age 50 and continuing through age 74, and women with underlying health histories/risks starting at age 40 (Appendix, 2019; Eguia et al., 2018). While The American College of Radiology recommends annual screening starting at age 40 and the private insurance covers the cost of one annual screening mammogram for average risk women starting at the age of 40 and is accepted by nearly all Iowa’s providers, the utilization rates may be an effect of the variations in recommendations for screening starting age and frequency (Vengalasetti et al., 2023). These low utilization rates may also indicate that some of these patients are seeking mammograms from providers that are not in-network with the private insurer, including seeking a mammogram provider in other states, thus, not appearing in our claims dataset. These rates discrepancies may also reflect differences utilization definition. The BRFSS reports utilization as the rate of women who reported to have received a mammogram in the past two years. Our study examined utilization rates for only screening mammograms, excluding diagnostic mammograms, and annually rather than in two-year increments. 

Utilization of mammography screening was lowest among women 40–49 and women with rural residence across the entire study period. Our findings of lower rate of utilization correspond to the literature that suggests that younger women and women in rural areas have lower utilization rates of mammography screening (Vengalasetti et al., 2023 Studies suggest that this could be due to spatial inaccessibility including longer drive distances. Longer drive times impede on family and

These concerns are exacerbated when discussing rural areas having less access and longer drive times to their appointments. However as stated above, Iowa had increased availability of mammography machines to rural areas, and most Iowans are able to drive less than 30 min to their mammography facility. Previous studies also found that not having a personal health care provider, unemployment, and cost as barriers for women 40–49 to receive screening mammograms (Miller et al., 2024b). Lower – utilization rates among women 40 49 may also in part be due to lower recommendation rates for providers for women under 50 years old compared to women older than 50. Studies have found that as there have been higher rates of false positive diagnosis of breast cancer among those women under 50 years old (Grimm et al., 2022). Along with the USPSTF recommendation for screening starting at age 50 biannually, providers may be recommending breast cancer screening less often for women 40–49. The USPSTF has since lowered the starting screening age to 40 biannually in 2024 in part from an increase in the rate of breast cancer diagnosis among women 40 to 49 (Nicholson et al., 2024). 

Temporally, we saw that utilization rates increased slightly from 2016 to 2019 (41.2 %–43.6 %) and then decreased by 12.5 percentage points in 2020, rebounding back to 44.1 % utilization of screening mammography in 2022. Studies examining breast cancer screening utilization after the COVID pandemic in 2020 across the US found similar patterns of as much as a 70 % decrease in utilization in 2020 but nearly a 91.8 % rebound in screening mammograms in the years following the pandemic (Grimm et al., 2022). Breast cancer screening in Iowa, specifically, among the privately insured women was more resilient throughout and following the pandemic, with even high utilization rates than prior to the pandemic.

We determined that suburban women and rural women are more likely to suffer from poor spatial accessibility; however, women who reside in rural and small-town areas of Iowa are less likely to utilize breast cancer screening. Studies suggest that improving the location and capacity of mammogram screening facilities in rural and small-town areas could reduce the driving distance barriers to obtaining screening mammograms especially in these rural areas potentially leading to increased breast cancer screening among the younger recommended breast cancer screening population of women (Young et al., 2020b; Heller et al., 2018). The use of mobile clinics or increasing providers and clinic operation capabilities would improve access and potentially utilization of screening mammograms especially within rural and small-town areas (Bakhai et al., 2024). 

Several of the areas in our model that indicate concern for spatial accessibility or having higher percentages of unallocated population, such as the western edge of Iowa, the Southeastern edge, west central and east central areas of the state may have concordance to the areas of higher risk probability for later stage breast cancer diagnosis from 2014 to 2018 according to the Iowa Cancer Registry. Public health campaigns or mobile mammography clinics could utilize these findings to focus on areas in the central-west and south-east, and southern areas of the state that are most likely to experience disparities of poor spatial accessibility, including lack of access to a household vehicle to travel larger distances (Haynes, 1991). This would help improve the spatial accessibility of these areas by reducing the barriers to care such as driving time by bringing screening to them.
