Introduction 

Inequity of access to community-based primary health care (PHC) services is a considerable health concern in Canada that is relatively unstudied in the context of local geographic scales. The Canada Health Act (CHA or the Act) has an explicit objective “to protect, promote and restore the physical and mental well-being of residents of Canada and to facilitate reasonable access to health services without financial or other barriers” (Canada 1985, C-6) It is aimed at the national health care system being truly universal and able to comprehensively administer accessible health services to all Canadians (Canada 1985, C-6; Knight et al. 2009). In the absence of a clearly defined accessibility principle (or threshold), reasonable access to needed and appropriate health services can be interpreted in many ways and still comply with the Act. These interpretations can be based on compositional and/ or contextual characteristics of predisposing, enabling, and need factors (Aday and Andersen 1974; Andersen et al. 1983; Babitsch et al. 2012). Evaluating geographic access to health services involves investigating distribution of these services in relation to population health needs and has important policy implications regarding health care delivery (Khan 1992; Luo and Wang 2003; Bissonnette et al. 2012). 

There are growing concerns in Canada that the health system is not as responsive as it could be for certain areas (and for certain populations) (Canada 

2001; Johnson et al. 2008), particularly those rural and urban areas where inequities in access to health care services exist. Moreover, rehabilitation services such as physiotherapy, in comparison to other PHC services provided by family physicians (FPs), receive less attention and seem to be under-recognized and under-valued (Barnes et al. 2012; Bath et al. 2015). The lack of attention and prioritization for access to rehabilitation professionals such as physiotherapists (PTs) creates a potentially vulnerable scenario whereby the growing health needs of an aging population and those with chronic conditions will not be adequately met (Chapman et al. 2003; Canizares et al. 2014; Badley et al. 2015). 

In Canada, PHC “recognizes the broader determinants of health and includes coordinating, integrating, and expanding systems and services to provide more population health, sickness prevention, and health promotion, not necessarily just by doctors” (Mable and Marriott 2002). In practice, the PHC model in Canada involves a broad range of health professionals such as nurses, nurse practitioners, physicians, dietitians, PTs, and social workers. Delivery of care varies across provincial/territorial health systems in many ways that include the range and configuration of services, governance, and funding models (IPAC [Institute of Public Administration of Canada] 2013; Allin and Rudoler 2015). Saskatchewan, like other provinces/territories in Canada, is in midst of PHC reform and a variety of initiatives such as support for inter-professional primary health care teams (The Conference Board of 

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473 

Spatial accessibility to health care services 463 

Canada 2012), group practices and networks, and financial incentives are under consideration. Such initiatives are expected to improve the access to PHC, especially in rural and remote communities with shortages of health care services (Hutchison et al. 2011; Bath 2012; Dufour et al. 2014). Physiotherapy services, like other PHC services, help people “to restore, maintain and maximize their strength, function, movement, and overall well-being” (SPA 2015, 1) and are provided in both public and private settings that may be variably funded by public and private sources (CPTA 2007; Bath 2012). Family physician services are predominantly funded through public sources (Health Canada 2012). 

To date, there has been little work in Canada or internationally, that has focused on investigating geographical barriers to accessing PT services, particularly in relation to other PHC providers such as FPs. Although there has been an increasing interest in geographic access to health care services to determine under-served areas and under-served populations (Guagliardo et al. 2004; Roeger et al. 2010; Ngui and Apparicio 2011; Bell et al. 2012; Crooks and Schuurman 2012; Bell et al. 2013; Sanders et al. 2013), most of this work has focused exclusively on physician services. 

The specific objectives of this study are: (1) to examine the variation in geographic accessibility to community-based PTs and FPs across Saskatchewan to identify under-serviced (or poorly served) rural and urban municipalities; (2) to analyze the patterns of spatial distribution of PT and FP services; and (3) explore the association of health provider distribution with socially disadvantaged population groups (or socio-demographic “need” characteristics) across Saskatchewan. 

Methods 

A geographical information system (GIS) approach was applied to examine the spatial distributions of community-based PTs and FPs across Saskatchewan and to explore these patterns in relation to socially disadvantaged groups. A location map illustrating study areas is shown in Figure 1. As the size and shape of the area being investigated (health regions, census units, neighbourhood, etc.) may produce different results depending on the chosen political/ administrative unit of study (Schuurman et al. 2007; 

Smiley et al. 2010; Bell et al. 2013; Shah et al. 2014a), we accepted census consolidated subdivisions (CCSs) (i.e., a group of adjacent municipalities or census subdivisions [CSDs])[1] as the most meaningful variable for both rural residents and planners to examine distribution of PT and FP services, as well as identify under-serviced areas. Based on the 2011 Canadian Census, there were 300 CCSs in Saskatchewan. We did not include four CCSs (i.e., approximately 50 percent of the Saskatchewan land area with only 5.1 percent of the Saskatchewan population) in the analysis due to their large geographic spread (area size > 5,000 km[2] ). One of these four CCSs is an unorganized territory that covers three regional health authorities (RHAs) (Athabasca; Keewatin Yatthe; Mamawetan Churchill River) and some parts of Prince Albert Parkland and Kelsey Trail RHAs (see, Figure 1). 

The following categories of physicians—family doctors, family physicians, general practitioners, and physiotherapists involved in direct patient care (in both public and private settings)—were used to calculate the FP and PT access scores. Data on the distribution of PTs (n ¼ 615) and FPs (n ¼ 1,171) were gathered from publicly available and routinely updated sources (i.e., provincial colleges of physicians and surgeons, Saskatchewan College of Physical Therapists). For this research, 982 out of 1,171 FPs were included in the analysis whereas physicians who were practicing in hospitals, working at non-practice locations (such as at academic institutions), or practicing outside the province are not included in the analysis (n ¼ 189). Physiotherapists who provide direct patient care outside of hospitals or acute care in both the public and private sector (558 out of total 615: public [301], private [220], and other [37]) are included. To map the health care services, an integrated geocoding approach using different reference datasets (i.e., road network layer, and multiple enhanced postal-code [MEP] points) was used to convert postal address/postalcode information into a set of geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude) (Bell et al. 2012; Shah et al. 2014b). 

Geographic accessibility to PT and FP services was measured by applying the Three-Steps Floating Catchment Areas (3SFCA) method (Luo 2004; 

> 1 http://www.statcan.gc.ca/pub/92-195-x/2011001/geo/ccs-sru/ ccs-sru-eng.htm

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473

Figure 1 

Population density (per km[2] ) of study area at Consolidated Census Subdivisions (CCSs). Note: RHA ¼ Regional Health Authority; n ¼ count of number of items. 

Bissonnette et al. 2012; Bell et al. 2013; Allan 2014) at the CCS level (municipality level) in a GIS environment. Generally, methods dealing with the geographic accessibility can be divided into two different groups (Joseph and Phillips 1984). One is related to regional availability of health care services with the assumption that regional boundaries are impermeable—suitable for large regions such as census divisions (Pong and Pitblado 2005), 

health areas (Thommasen and Thommasen 2001; Olatunde 2007), and utilization-based service areas (Shipman et al. 2010). The second group is based on the spatial interaction processes (e.g., distance decay) that manipulate supply and demand data at local scales using gravity models and kernel density estimations (Joseph and Phillips 1984; Luo and Wang 2003; Guagliardo 2004; Schuurman et al. 2010; Cromley and McLafferty 2012; McGrail 2012; 

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473 

Spatial accessibility to health care services 465 

Wang 2012; Neutens 2015). The 3SFCA method applied in this research is a gravity model that is grounded in the use of GIS technologies to enhance research in medical geography by integrating spatial and non-spatial attribute information in one system to analyze relationships and conduct complex computational tasks related to spatial data (Wang and Luo 2005; Bissonnette et al. 2012; McGrail 2012; Delamater 2013; Passalent et al. 2013; Fransen et al. 2015; Wang and Onega 2015). The 3SFCA method was applied to examine geographic distribution of PT and FP services (i.e., supply) with respect to the population (i.e., demand). An access score (PT and FP services-per-1000 population) was calculated using a 25 km catchment area. In health geography, there is no consensus on the catchment distances/areas (Allan 2014; McGrail and Humphreys 2014). The selection of 25 km for catchment areas is based on the premise that catchment area should be greater than the average area of the areal units used as a unit of analysis in this study (CCS Area: Mean ¼ 993 km[2] ; SD ¼ 480 km[2] ) which takes into account that individuals in one municipality may seek care in other municipalities. A series of catchment areas ranging from 5 km to 40 km (with a 5 km increment) was explored in order to pick the appropriate catchment area size (25 km). The 3SFCA method is based on the approach that involves more complex calculations of access using spatial proximity of supply and demand data at local scales (Joseph and Phillips 1984; Luo and Wang 2003; Guagliardo 2004; Schuurman et al. 2010; Wang 2012). Supporting datasets were accessed through the GIS Library Services, part of The Spatial Initiative at the University of Saskatchewan that provides access to geospatial data, GIS software, and research services. These datasets included a digital geographic boundary file for health regions; demographic data; a digital geographic file of the 2011 Canadian Census and National Household Survey (NHS) at dissemination area (DA) and CSD geographic scales; and a road network for geo-coding purposes. 

The 3SFCA places a 25 km catchment around a point of health care supply to identify the DAs within it and then estimate a provider-to-population ratio (dividing number of health care providers at that location by total population of identified DAs). Next, a second catchment (25 km radius) around a point of population demand (DA centroids) was used to identify the health care providers within it and to sum the provider-to-population ratios. A 

third step introduced to avoid the methodological inaccuracies related to unit of analysis (Nykiforuk and Flaman 2008; Shah et al. 2014a) was used to estimate the access ratio at municipality (CCS) by averaging the provider-to-population ratios for all DAs falling within a municipality. 

In brief, an accessibility score for both FP and PT services at municipality (CCS) level that characterizes the geographic accessibility to health care services was estimated using a 25 km catchment and a set of maps showing the spatial distributions of the 3SFCA access score was produced (see Figures 2 and 3). To compare the distributions of PT and FP services in association with population groups with potentially high health care needs and those facing barriers in accessing health care services (such as geographic, socio-cultural, and economic barriers) (Asanin and Wilson 2008), bivariate/cross tabulation analyses were performed. The following variables were identified from the 2011 Population Census and National Household Survey (NHS) for the cross tab analysis: (1) low income individuals below the after-tax low-income measure (LIM-AT); (2) population aged 65þ; (3) population aged 15þ without certificate, diploma, or degree; (4) immigrants who came to Canada from 2001 to 2011; and (5) aboriginal ethnicity. These need variables were selected by considering the contextual factors presented in the following studies (Field 2000; Andersen and Davidson 2001; Teng et al. 2007; Asanin and Wilson 2008; Pampalon et al. 2012) as well as availability of data. It is also accepted that the proportion of the population aged over 65 years will generally place greater demands on the health care system (Christensen et al. 2009; CIHI 2011). The LIMAT grouping variable was chosen as there is evidence to suggest that people with lower socioeconomic status often have poorer health outcomes (Rawshani et al. 2015). While there is evidence for a “healthy immigrant effect” where immigrants are known to be healthier in their first 10 years of settlement (McDonald and Kennedy 2004), there are also known substantial barriers for accessibility of health services by immigrants. These include lack of service awareness due to language barriers, sociocultural demands, and economic demands in terms of access to private PTs (Asanin and Wilson 2008). The CSD level census data were used to prepare the CCS variables. The following software was used for mapping and data analysis (spatial and non-spatial): ArcMap 10.3, SPSS 22, Microsoft Access 2013, and

Results 

The results show substantial variations in spatial distributions of PT and FP services across Saskatchewan and highlight which areas are poorly served. The spatial patterns of, and differences between, the 3SFCA access scores for PT and FP services are shown in Figures 2 and 3. In both measures, higher numbers (access scores) represent better geographic access to health services (i.e., health care providers-per-1000 population). The access scores for both FP and PT services are categorized into four 

manually defined classes: <0.01; 0.01––0.51; 0.52–– 1.01; >1.01. This is done to emphasize: (1) a particular range of values, above or below threshold value such as one health care provider-per-1000 — population a measure that may be more meaningful to health planners, policy makers, and local residents; and (2) to allow greater means of comparison between the health care provider groups. The first class, <0.01 (CCSs with the lowest access scores or health care provider-per-1000 population ratio), indicates poorly served municipalities. Provincial access scores for PT and FP –– services are 0.46 (range: 0.00 1.88) and 0.85 (range: 0.00––2.44), respectively. In both cases, a typical distribution pattern can be seen where higher access scores are associated with urban municipalities with decreasing accessibility in more rural and remote municipalities. Comparatively, the PT access 

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473 

468 Tayyab I. Shah, Brenna Bath, and Stephan Milosavljevic 

score across Saskatchewan is lower than the FP access score. The differences between PT and FP measures are shown in Figure 3 where all municipalities (n ¼ 296) are categorized into three classes: municipalities where the difference is within the range of 0.50 health care providers-per-1000 population (n ¼ 211); where FP > PT with margin of 0.50 scores (n ¼ 81); where PT > FP with margin of 0.50 scores (n ¼ 4). There are only four rural municipalities where PT access scores are better than FP (see Figure 3). On the other hand, there are a large number of rural and urban municipalities (e.g., Moose Jaw, Swift Current, and North Battleford) where FP access scores are better than those for PT. 

We used a cross tabulation analysis approach to examine the relationship between the distribution of PT and FP services in Saskatchewan. Table 1 presents population distributions and frequencies of the geographic areal units (municipality/CCS) by four PT and FP access categories: (1) percent of the Saskatchewan population; (2) proportional population (count); and (3) CCS count. Table 1 shows that there is a greater proportion of the Saskatchewan population that has lower access scores for PT in comparison to FP (15.2 percent vs. 9.7 percent, respectively). Table 2 presents the results of the cross tabulation analysis of population groups with high health needs and those facing barriers in accessing the health care services by PT and FP access categories. In each population groups, each cell presents the proportion (%) of the total group population along with the proportion of the whole population in that cell. Interestingly, when comparing the poorly served categories of PT and FP access scores, a considerable proportion of the Saskatchewan population has poor PT access in all five –– selected variables (see Table 2 a f). For example, of 

the 148,730 people age 65 and over, approximately 7 percent more population have poor geographic accessibility for PT services (PT: 18.2 percent versus FP: 11.2 percent). 

Discussion 

The objectives of this study were to examine the variation in the geographic accessibility to community-based PT and FP services and explore the patterns of spatial distribution of these services in association with socially disadvantaged groups in order to identify poorly served municipalities across Saskatchewan. Our results show a difference between the distributions of PT and FP services across Saskatchewan and highlight the municipalities where PT services in general, as well as in comparison to FP services, are far less equitable than other municipalities. While natural variance between community distributions of PTs and FPs is expected—given differences in work tasks and — health care responsibilities Figure 3 demonstrates that 27 percent of communities have substantially greater FP coverage than PTs (> 0.50 access score). The remaining 73 percent of communities are likely to have adequate comparative support from both FPs and PTs (þ/� 0.50 access score). The reasons for 27 percent of communities having poorer relative accessibility to PT services (compared to FP) is uncertain and beyond the scope of this study. Further research should focus on this disparity and identify those variables that offer the best explanation for this observation. While we have considered age, income, education, immigration, and ethnicity for this study there is a need to identify further variables and composites of

We also found that a large volume of population with potentially high health care needs such as the population age 65 and over, low income individuals, and those facing barriers in accessing the health 

care services including recent immigrants and those with aboriginal status/ethnicity, have reduced access to PT services particularly in rural and remote municipalities in comparison to FP services. 

Proportion of seniors (population age 65 and over): This variable represents the proportion of seniors age 65 and above (148,000 which is 15.4 percent of the Saskatchewan population) within a municipality. Increased age is often associated with higher potential need for both FP and PT services 

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473 

470 Tayyab I. Shah, Brenna Bath, and Stephan Milosavljevic 

(Beaudet et al. 2013; Canizares et al. 2014). About 10 percent of the population age 65 and over in Saskatchewan is facing poor accessibility to both PT and FP services, with a greater proportion of the population facing limited access to PT services. As the Canadian population is aging, spatial accessibility to FP and PT services will likely be more and more challenging. 

Low income was defined as individuals that belong to the economic families below the Statistics Canada low-income measure (LIM-AT) at a municipality/CSD scale. According to the 2011 census, 13.6 percent of the total Saskatchewan population falls under this low-income category (see Table 2). The inequalities between the distributions of PT and FP services across Saskatchewan in relation to low income is not different from the population of seniors. PT services, unlike FP services in Saskatchewan and elsewhere in Canada, are not fully covered through the public provincial health plan. Furthermore, public PT services are generally known to have long wait times, and the costs of private PT services may be prohibitive if people do not have third-party health insurance coverage. All of these factors suggest that spatial accessibility to PT services is problematic and inequity of access is strongly evident for certain population groups and defined geographic areas. Socially disadvantaged groups represented by the variables population 15 years and older having no high school level education, recent immigrants (who came to Canada from 2001 to 2011), and aboriginal status population may be associated with reduced potential access to health care services (Reading and Wien 2009). Higher proportions of these population groups within rural and remote areas point to additional challenges in access to both FP and PT services beyond geographical barriers. 

This comparative analysis between the spatial distributions of PTs and FPs demonstrates where geographical access to these services is reduced in both rural and urban Saskatchewan communities. This information contributes to a better understanding of accessibility to PT and FP services which may inform health workforce planning policies and strategies at provincial, health region, and local levels. However, more research on the geographic accessibility to PHC services across large urban centres and census metropolitan influenced zones (MIZs)—and how the variation between them relates 

to existing provincial health policy, population health care needs, aboriginal health disparity, and health care costs—would be worthwhile. 

Low geographic accessibility may either be linked to a lack of health human resources within a community, or an inability to access health care services by disadvantaged populations. It is of interest to observe that access to PT services is considerably more disparate than access to FP services. Health conditions that would benefit from physiotherapy care are often long term or chronic in nature (e.g., stroke rehabilitation, recovery from orthopaedic surgery, management of chronic pain, and mobility issues) and inequity of access in these observed geographical locations is likely to result in sub-optimal recovery and reduced quality of life and adversely influence the overall health of the community. While provision and access to some services in Canada are clearly defined (e.g., education catchment areas) there appears to be no clearly defined guidance on what constitutes an appropriate health catchment (or service) area. While we consider our choice of a 25 km areal unit to have been appropriate for the geospatial parameters of Saskatchewan, based on the average municipal size, there is little evidence to indicate that this is the most sensitive or likely measure applicable to other Canadian populations or jurisdictions. Thus there is currently little evidence for cross-jurisdictional acceptance of benchmarks for appropriate geographic accessibility. 

Some important limitations of this study are related to the selection criteria for the FPs and PTs, and the procedures used for data preparation. The exclusion of samples with non-geocodable addresses may have introduced bias. The DA representative coordinates for the 2011 DAs (i.e., health care demands) generated by Statistics Canada’s Spatial Data Infrastructure and the PHC service locations generated using integrated geocoding may have positional errors which could influence the 3SFCA scores (GeoSuite 2012). These findings may be somewhat limited by the fact that the funding mechanisms for PT vary compared to those for FPs. This study did not present information on public/private PT practice types, which means that some socioeconomically disadvantaged groups may not have adequate or equitable access to PT due to cost factors (and not just because of geographical factors). 

The Canadian Geographer / Le Geographe[�] canadien 2015, 59(4): 461–473 

Spatial accessibility to health care services 471 

This study contributes to the application of GIS technologies to health services accessibility; highlighting PT and FP accessibility issues across Saskatchewan; identification of possible gaps in PHC services to be addressed in concert with health decision-makers; and advancing current provincial and national efforts to reform PHC. It also provides evidence to inform progressive, comprehensive, and accessible PHC, all fundamental to actualizing the Act and benefiting the health of Saskatchewan residents. These findings further support the idea of integrating publicly funded PTs with the other PHC service providers such as FPs and/or nurse practitioners to improve population health and chronic disease management, particularly in areas of high potential need (Dufour et al. 2014).
